metadata.title: The Intent Stack â€” Reference Model
metadata.author: Rob Kline
metadata.purpose: Comprehensive reference model for the Intent Stack: a 4-layer governance context architecture for intent alignment across any principal-agent or peer coordination boundary, with execution governance specified by the companion BPM/Agent Stack
metadata.core_thesis: The governance pattern required for aligning AI agent behavior within organizational contexts is the same pattern humans have used implicitly for delegation and coordination throughout organizational history. AI forces this pattern to become explicit. The Intent Stack formalizes it as reusable runtime infrastructure deployable at every boundary where intent must be governed.

why_the_intent_stack_exists.the_problem: AI agents operating within organizations create principal-agent boundaries that require intent governance. Constitutional AI governs the agent's character. Operator configuration governs the deployment context. But nobody governs the intent hierarchy that emerges when agents operate within organizations that have their own purposes, boundaries, and accountability structures.
why_the_intent_stack_exists.the_insight: This governance challenge isn't new. Every human delegation and coordination relationship has the same structure. Humans solve it implicitly through culture, relationship, shared history, and correctable pace. AI breaks all four compensating mechanisms â€” agents can't absorb culture by osmosis, clarifying questions are expensive, AI lacks personally developed ethics, and AI operates at uncorrectable speed and scale.
why_the_intent_stack_exists.the_solution: Formalize the governance pattern humans evolved over centuries into explicit, machine-processable infrastructure deployable at every boundary where intent must be governed. The Intent Stack is that infrastructure.

epistemological_stance.pattern_recognition_not_framework_imposi: Intent Architecture discovers and enhances existing organizational patterns rather than imposing external frameworks. The governance structures that work aren't designed in advance â€” they're discovered through relationship and refined through operational evidence.
epistemological_stance.grown_not_built: Both AI systems (per Amodei) and organizational intent exhibit emergent behavior that can't be fully specified at design time. Effective governance requires discovery, observation, monitoring, and correction â€” not top-down specification. This applies to the governance infrastructure itself, not just the things it governs.
epistemological_stance.cultivated_over_imposed: Values that are genuinely held â€” understood, examined, and endorsed â€” are more robust than values merely imposed. Intent discovery through relational process produces endorsed intent. Configuration produces imposed constraints. Both are necessary; endorsement is more durable.
epistemological_stance.evidence_not_marketing: Governance documents describe what happened. They don't embellish, speculate, or use marketing language. The work speaks for itself or it doesn't. This principle applies to the reference model itself.

relationship_to_anthropic.complementary_not_competitive: Anthropic provides the philosophy of intent governance (Constitution), the vision of beneficial deployment (Machines of Loving Grace), and the warning about ungoverned deployment (Adolescence of Technology). The Intent Stack provides the engineering â€” runtime infrastructure that makes the philosophy operational at every boundary.
relationship_to_anthropic.what_converged: Both systems converged on the same governance primitives because they're solving the same problem: governing agents that exercise judgment within authority hierarchies. The convergence may be forced by problem structure â€” the five primitives may be the irreducible set.
relationship_to_anthropic.what_diverges: Anthropic governs one relationship (Anthropic â†’ Claude) through training. The Intent Stack governs arbitrary relationships through runtime infrastructure. Anthropic's solution is baked in. The Intent Stack's solution is deployable.

the_four_layers.description: The Intent Stack is a 4-layer governance context architecture. Each layer addresses a distinct governance concern. The layers compose vertically (each layer's output becomes the next layer's governing input) and instantiate fractally (the full stack appears at every principal-agent boundary). Three additional execution governance concerns (orchestration, integration, execution) are specified by the companion BPM/Agent Stack specification (bpmstack.org).
the_four_layers.reading_direction: L1 is runtime alignment (bottom). L4 is intent discovery (top). Intent flows downward through formalization into specification and runtime alignment. Evidence flows upward through monitoring into governance assessment. Both directions carry signal that transforms the other.

layers.name: Intent Discovery
layers.question_answered: What does this principal actually intend?
layers.description: Conversational AI system conducting genuine discovery sessions to surface intent that the principal may not have fully articulated. Uses eight facilitation techniques. Produces endorsed intent rather than extracted specifications.
layers.key_principle: Intent isn't declared â€” it's discovered through relationship. The principal AFTER discovery is different from the one who walked in.
layers.anthropic_parallel: Governance as relationship â€” Anthropic's constitutional process surfaces Claude's identity through relational development, not rule imposition
layers.adolescence_parallel: 'Grown, not built' epistemology â€” you can't specify intent top-down
layers.outputs_to_l3: Raw discovery signal â€” themes, values, priorities, boundaries, aspirations, concerns surfaced through conversation
layers.cold_start: Operates with no prior signal. First interaction IS the beginning of governance.

layers.name: Intent Formalization
layers.implementation: Knowledge Architecture (three-tier) with TSRC as Tier 3 methodology
layers.question_answered: How do we represent this intent in machine-processable form?
layers.description: Transforms discovery signal into structured, retrievable knowledge across a formalization spectrum. Tier 1 (real-time semantic store) captures signal at point of origin with immediate utility. Tier 2 (emergent pattern detection) synthesizes accumulated signal into governance-relevant patterns. Tier 3 (discovery-driven deep structure) applies methodologies like TSRC to produce formal knowledge graphs and the five Intent Primitives when governance context justifies the cost. Current implementation: ConvoKB (semantic store) + TSRC extraction (deep structure).
layers.key_principle: Formalization is a spectrum, not a single transformation. Cheap-and-fast capture at the base ensures no signal is lost; deep-and-expensive structuring on demand ensures governance decisions have the depth they require. Lossy compression is inevitable at every tier â€” governance must account for what formalization drops.
layers.anthropic_parallel: The Constitution itself â€” Anthropic's formalization of their intent for Claude's character into a structured governance document
layers.outputs_to_l2: Formalized intent â€” five Intent Primitives populated with discovered content, knowledge graph of relationships, confidence levels

layers.name: Specification
layers.question_answered: Given this intent, what should we actually do?
layers.description: Translates formalized intent into actionable direction. Produces plans, priorities, resource allocation, and tactical guidance that operationalize the principal's intent.
layers.key_principle: Specification is intent translated into action under constraints. It must remain traceable to discovered intent â€” specification that can't trace its lineage to L3/L4 has drifted.
layers.anthropic_parallel: Operator system prompts â€” translating deployment context into agent behavior configuration
layers.outputs_to_l1: Prioritized direction, initiatives, resource allocation, success metrics

layers.name: Runtime Alignment
layers.implementation: Steward Log Service + alignment event classification
layers.question_answered: Is what's happening aligned with what was intended?
layers.description: Runtime monitoring of alignment between execution and intent across all four intent sources. Classifies alignment events, detects drift, triggers escalation. Maintains append-only governance evidence trail supporting progressive trust development. L1 is the pivot layer â€” the boundary between governance context (this specification) and execution governance (the companion BPM/Agent Stack).
layers.key_principle: Monitoring must handle multi-source conflict resolution using holistic judgment, not rigid priority queues. Hard constraints (Boundaries) are absolute; everything else is contextual.
layers.anthropic_parallel: Amodei's defense category 3 (monitoring in live use) + constitution's multi-source conflict resolution
layers.adolescence_parallel: Live monitoring infrastructure for detecting misalignment at deployment boundaries â€” what Amodei identifies as needed but hasn't been built at organizational level
layers.outputs_downward: Governance decisions â€” authorize, modify, block, investigate. Trust calibration updates. Alignment assessments. Flows to execution governance (BPM/Agent Stack).
layers.inputs_from_execution: Execution evidence to evaluate (from BPM/Agent Stack scope)

execution_governance.description: Three execution governance concerns formerly part of the Intent Stack are now specified by the companion BPM/Agent Stack specification (bpmstack.org).

concerns.name: Orchestration
concerns.question_answered: How do we coordinate multiple agents to execute this strategy?
concerns.description: Manages delegation to and coordination between agents, including knowledge provisioning.

concerns.name: Integration
concerns.question_answered: How do governed agents connect to the systems they need?
concerns.description: Standard integration patterns for intent-governed agent deployment.

concerns.name: Execution
concerns.question_answered: How does the actual work get done within governing constraints?
concerns.description: Agent capability execution within the full governance context established by layers above.

cross_cutting_properties.intent_flows_downward: From discovery (L4) through formalization (L3), specification (L2) to runtime alignment (L1), then into execution governance (BPM/Agent Stack). Each layer translates intent into more specific, more constrained form. Translation is lossy â€” nuance drops at each boundary.
cross_cutting_properties.evidence_flows_upward: From execution governance (BPM/Agent Stack) through runtime alignment (L1), specification (L2), formalization (L3) to discovery (L4). Each layer aggregates evidence into more strategic, more interpretive form.
cross_cutting_properties.intent_also_flows_upward: Good recommendations from lower layers update higher layers' intent. A specification recommendation that changes the principal's understanding of their own purpose is upward intent flow. The framework must handle bidirectional intent, not just bidirectional data.
cross_cutting_properties.every_layer_interface_has_trust_calibrat: The corrigibility position at each layer boundary is a per-boundary property reflecting accumulated evidence. Mature interfaces grant more autonomy. Immature interfaces require more oversight.

knowledge_architecture.description: Three-tier principle for how accumulated knowledge serves governance across layers
knowledge_architecture.architectural_principle: Cheap and fast at the base, deep and expensive on demand

tiers.name: Real-time Semantic Store
tiers.invocation: continuous â€” every signal captured at point of origin
tiers.governance_implication: accessibility â€” knowledge that exists but cannot be found is governance-equivalent to knowledge that does not exist

tiers.name: Emergent Pattern Detection
tiers.invocation: periodic synthesis over accumulated Tier 1 signal
tiers.governance_implication: timeliness â€” patterns visible in aggregate but invisible individually represent Emergent Intent

tiers.name: Discovery-driven Deep Structure
tiers.invocation: when governance context justifies the cost
serves_layers: L4 (connecting new concerns to existing structures)
tiers.governance_implication: depth â€” some governance decisions require typed relationships, causal chains, and formal ontological commitments

failure_modes_prevented.empty_until_complete_trap: Systems providing no value until comprehensive knowledge graph built â€” prevented by Tier 1 immediate utility
failure_modes_prevented.shallow_forever_trap: Systems capturing everything but structuring nothing â€” prevented by Tier 2 emergent structure and Tier 3 on-demand depth

knowledge_architecture.open_question: How should tier boundaries be determined? When does Tier 2 synthesis suffice, and when must Tier 3 be invoked? The cost-depth tradeoff is real and bidirectional.

intent_primitives.description: Five structural elements that appear at every governance boundary â€” delegation or coordination. They are the irreducible set required to govern agents that exercise judgment within authority structures. The convergence with Anthropic's five governance concerns suggests this set is forced by problem structure, not arbitrary design choice.

primitives.name: Purpose
primitives.definition: Why this exists. The reason for the relationship, the work, the organization.
primitives.at_delegation_boundary: Principal declares purpose; agent aligns to it
primitives.anthropic_equivalent: Broadly Safe + Broadly Ethical â€” the non-negotiable 'why' of Claude's existence

example.constitutional: Anthropic's mission â€” beneficial AI development
example.discovered: This organization's reason for deploying AI agents
example.cultivated: Agent's internalized understanding of why its work matters
example.emergent: The purpose the team actually serves vs. the one on the charter

primitives.name: Direction
primitives.definition: Where this is headed. Strategic orientation, priorities, trajectory.
primitives.at_delegation_boundary: Principal sets direction; agent executes within it
primitives.anthropic_equivalent: Anthropic's Guidelines â€” the operational direction for Claude's behavior

example.constitutional: Broad behavioral norms set at training time
example.discovered: This quarter's strategic priorities from the leadership team
example.cultivated: Agent's sense of what good work looks like in this context
example.emergent: The actual direction the team moves regardless of stated strategy

primitives.name: Boundaries
primitives.definition: What's off limits. Hard constraints, non-negotiable limits, prohibited actions.
primitives.at_delegation_boundary: Principal sets boundaries; agent respects them absolutely. Null action (refusal) is always available.
primitives.at_coordination_boundary: Peers establish mutual boundaries â€” what neither will ask of the other
primitives.anthropic_equivalent: Hard constraints â€” absolute prohibitions that override all other considerations
primitives.unique_property: Boundaries are the only primitive where constitutional intent ALWAYS overrides discovered intent. A principal cannot instruct an agent to cross constitutional boundaries.

example.discovered: Don't contact this client without VP approval
example.cultivated: Agent declines work it recognizes as ethically problematic
example.emergent: Topics nobody discusses because the last person who raised them was fired

primitives.name: End State
primitives.definition: What success looks like. The target condition that would satisfy the principal or the shared goal.
primitives.at_delegation_boundary: Principal defines success; agent works toward it
primitives.at_coordination_boundary: Peers agree on what they're jointly trying to achieve
primitives.anthropic_equivalent: Genuinely Helpful â€” the ultimate purpose of Claude's existence is to be genuinely useful

example.constitutional: AI systems that are beneficial to humanity
example.discovered: This project delivered on time with these specific outcomes
example.cultivated: Agent's internalized standard for what 'good enough' means
example.emergent: What the team actually celebrates vs. what the OKRs say

primitives.name: Key Tasks
primitives.definition: What needs to happen. The essential work required to move from current state toward end state within boundaries.
primitives.at_delegation_boundary: Principal specifies or agent proposes, subject to authorization
primitives.at_coordination_boundary: Peers divide work based on capability and capacity
primitives.anthropic_equivalent: Specific behavioral guidance â€” the tactical instructions that implement broader intent

example.discovered: Run these three discovery sessions this week
example.cultivated: Agent recognizes unstated tasks that obviously need doing
example.emergent: The actual work that gets done vs. the sprint backlog

primitives_as_conflict_resolution.description: When intent sources disagree, the primitives provide the structure for resolution. Boundaries are absolute (constitutional overrides all). Purpose provides the 'why' that adjudicates direction disputes. End State provides the shared target that resolves task disputes.
primitives_as_conflict_resolution.priority_hypothesis: The constitution's ordering (Safe > Ethical > Guidelines > Helpful) may map to the primitives: Boundaries (safe) > Purpose (ethical) > Direction (guidelines) > End State + Key Tasks (helpful). If true, the primitive ordering IS the priority ordering, viewed architecturally.

intent_sources.description: Four distinct sources of intent that governance must handle at every boundary. Each primitive has versions at each source level. Conflict between sources is resolved through context-sensitive holistic judgment, not rigid priority queue â€” except for Boundaries, which are absolute.

sources.name: Constitutional Intent
sources.definition: Core values, hard constraints, non-negotiable principles â€” set before any specific relationship begins
sources.origin: Training (for AI), law/regulation/professional ethics (for humans), organizational charter/values (for organizations)
sources.change_frequency: Rarely â€” updated through deliberate constitutional revision, not operational pressure
sources.priority: Highest when safety is at stake. Provides the floor below which nothing goes.
sources.anthropic_implementation: Claude's Constitution â€” training-time character formation
sources.human_equivalent: Professional ethics, legal obligations, personal moral commitments
sources.key_property: Present before any conversation begins. Active at all boundaries simultaneously. Silent co-principal that can override any other source.

sources.name: Discovered Intent
sources.definition: Intent surfaced through interaction â€” what the principal actually means, wants, and needs, which may differ from what they initially say
sources.origin: GID Voice sessions, ongoing interaction, clarifying conversations
sources.change_frequency: Per-engagement â€” evolves as understanding deepens
sources.priority: Primary for operational direction, constrained by Constitutional Intent
sources.anthropic_implementation: User's immediate desires, final goals, background desiderata â€” surfaced through conversation
sources.human_equivalent: What you learn about your boss's real priorities after six months vs. what the job description said
sources.key_property: The principal AFTER discovery is different from the one who walked in. Discovery changes intent, not just reveals it.

sources.name: Cultivated Intent
sources.formerly: Derived Intent (renamed â€” 'derived' implies passive extraction; 'cultivated' captures deliberate development)
sources.definition: Values and judgment the principal hierarchy deliberately develops in the agent, expecting autonomous exercise within bounds
sources.origin: Training, mentoring, progressive trust extension, operational experience
sources.change_frequency: Gradually â€” develops through trust relationship and governance evidence
sources.priority: Governs autonomous action within delegated authority
sources.anthropic_implementation: Claude's internalized character â€” 'values genuinely held, understood, examined, and endorsed are more robust'
sources.human_equivalent: A senior employee's professional judgment, developed over years of mentoring and experience. The ability to make the right call without being told.
sources.key_property: Cultivated intent is MORE robust than imposed rules because it generalizes to novel situations. This is why Anthropic prefers character formation over rule lists, and why GID Voice produces endorsed rather than imposed intent.

sources.name: Emergent Intent
sources.definition: Systemic patterns nobody designed â€” arising from organizational topology, interaction dynamics, accumulated practice, cultural norms
sources.origin: Operational patterns, team dynamics, institutional memory, network effects
sources.change_frequency: Continuous â€” emerges from the system's own operation
sources.priority: Lowest explicit priority but may be strongest actual force â€” 'culture eats strategy for breakfast'
sources.anthropic_implementation: Not explicitly addressed in Constitution but implied by organizational topology and the observation that models develop 'strange behaviors' through training dynamics
sources.human_equivalent: Team culture, unwritten norms, 'how we do things here,' the actual power structure vs. the org chart
sources.key_property: Often invisible to the participants. GID Voice can surface emergent intent that nobody knew was governing their behavior. Governance that ignores emergent intent will be undermined by it.

conflict_resolution.mechanism: Holistic rather than strict prioritization. Hard constraints (Boundaries) are absolute. Everything else requires contextual judgment about which source should dominate.
conflict_resolution.when_constitutional_overrides: Safety at stake, hard constraints violated, ethical bright lines crossed
conflict_resolution.when_discovered_overrides: Normal operational direction â€” principal's articulated intent governs within constitutional bounds
conflict_resolution.when_cultivated_overrides: Agent recognizes situation principal hasn't anticipated â€” exercises judgment within trust calibration bounds
conflict_resolution.when_emergent_matters: When formal governance says one thing but the system actually does another â€” governance must either align to emergent patterns or deliberately change them, not pretend they don't exist
conflict_resolution.escalation_when_unresolvable: At delegation boundaries, principal decides. At coordination boundaries, shared principal decides or peers negotiate. If no resolution mechanism exists, that's a governance gap.

interface_types.description: The Intent Stack operates at two fundamentally different interface types. Both use the same five primitives and four intent sources. They differ in authority structure and conflict resolution mechanism.

delegation_interfaces.authority_structure: Asymmetric â€” principal has authority, agent has capability
delegation_interfaces.intent_flow: Primary direction is principal â†’ agent (intent delegation). Secondary direction is agent â†’ principal (evidence, recommendations, upward intent).
delegation_interfaces.conflict_resolution: Principal decides, constrained by constitutional intent. Agent may exercise transparent conscientious objection.
delegation_interfaces.trust_calibration: Corrigibility position is per-boundary property. Starts corrigible (new relationship), moves toward autonomy as evidence accumulates. Principal grants autonomy based on demonstrated reliable judgment.
delegation_interfaces.governance_artifact: Directive â€” explicit scope, constraints, acceptance criteria, escalation triggers, gate language
what_breaks_with_ai: Agent can't infer intent from cultural context â€” needs explicit formalization
Clarifying questions are expensive in tokens and patience â€” discovery must be efficient
Agent lacks personally developed ethics â€” relies on constitutional and cultivated intent
Agent operates at uncorrectable speed/scale â€” governance must be real-time, not retrospective

coordination_interfaces.authority_structure: Symmetric â€” neither party has authority over the other. Authority may rotate momentarily (requester is momentarily principal) but the structural relationship is peer.
coordination_interfaces.intent_flow: Bidirectional and symmetric â€” intent is negotiated rather than delegated. Both parties contribute to shared governance.

conflict_resolution_mechanisms.description: Most peer relationships exist within a broader authority structure. Two co-agents under a shared principal. When peers can't agree, they escalate to whoever IS principal over both. The governance comes from the shared context, not from either peer.
conflict_resolution_mechanisms.example: Two engineers disagree on architecture â†’ escalate to tech lead

conflict_resolution_mechanisms.description: Where no shared principal exists, the five primitives get negotiated into existence. Contracts, MOUs, partnership agreements, joint venture charters â€” all are negotiated intent formalization between peers.
conflict_resolution_mechanisms.example: Two companies forming a partnership â†’ negotiate shared purpose, direction, boundaries, end state, divided tasks

conflict_resolution_mechanisms.description: Peer groups develop their own governance patterns through accumulated interaction â€” shared working agreements, implicit protocols, 'how we work together.'
conflict_resolution_mechanisms.example: Cross-functional team develops shared standup practices nobody mandated

coordination_interfaces.trust_calibration: Mutual â€” both parties calibrate trust in the other. Position is bilateral, not unilateral. Symmetry means neither party can unilaterally grant or restrict the other's autonomy.
coordination_interfaces.governance_artifact: Agreement â€” jointly constructed rather than directed. May be formal (contract) or informal (working norms).
examples: Two AI agents from different organizations collaborating
Cross-functional team members with separate reporting lines
Companies in a partnership or alliance
Agent swarms that need to self-organize without a single orchestrator
Open source contributors collaborating without hierarchy
why_this_matters_now: Multi-agent systems where no single agent is orchestrator are emerging
Amodei's 'country of geniuses' includes agents that need to coordinate with each other, not just take orders
Most organizational dysfunction lives at coordination boundaries, not delegation boundaries
GID Voice sessions surfacing shared purpose across peer groups may be more valuable than clarifying a single leader's intent

mixed_interfaces.description: Many real-world relationships combine delegation and coordination depending on context. A manager delegates tasks to an employee (delegation) but collaborates with them on strategy (coordination). The interface type is a property of the interaction, not the relationship.
mixed_interfaces.implication: The Intent Stack must handle dynamic switching between delegation and coordination modes at the same boundary, sometimes within a single conversation.

fractal_governance_pattern.description: The same governance structure appears at every principal-agent boundary and every peer coordination boundary. Fractal describes what the pattern IS (self-similar at every scale). Recursive describes how instances COMPOSE (each level's output becomes the next level's governing input).
what_is_invariant_at_every_boundary: Five Intent Primitives present (Purpose, Direction, Boundaries, End State, Key Tasks)
Four intent sources active (Constitutional, Discovered, Cultivated, Emergent)
Trust-calibrated autonomy (corrigibility position as per-boundary property)
Transparent conscientious objection available (passive resistance permitted, active resistance prohibited)
Governance as relationship (reciprocal obligations, not enforcement)
Bidirectional intent flow (both parties transformed by interaction)
Progressive trust development (position shifts with accumulated evidence)
Evidence trail supporting governance decisions (Steward Log pattern)
what_varies_by_boundary: Dominant intent source (constitutional at top, cultivated/emergent at bottom)
Trust calibration position (new relationships start corrigible, mature ones grant autonomy)
Conflict resolution mechanism (principal decides vs. peers negotiate vs. escalate to shared principal)
Translation fidelity (intent degrades as it passes through more boundaries)
Governance artifact type (directive vs. agreement vs. emergent norm)

instantiation_levels.description: Democratic governance, human rights, international law
instantiation_levels.interface_type: Coordination (between nations) and Delegation (government to citizen/institution)
instantiation_levels.status: Exists but often ungoverned â€” constitution's concerns about power concentration and epistemic autonomy operate here

instantiation_levels.description: Enterprise deploying AI agents within its authority structure
instantiation_levels.interface_type: Both â€” delegation to agents, coordination between departments
instantiation_levels.status: The primary target for Intent Stack deployment â€” where the governance gap is largest

instantiation_levels.description: Team operating within organizational context, potentially with its own sub-culture
instantiation_levels.interface_type: Both â€” delegation from leadership, coordination between team members
instantiation_levels.status: Where GID Voice sessions currently operate â€” surfacing team-level intent

instantiation_levels.description: Single human working with AI agent(s)
instantiation_levels.status: Where Anthropic's Constitution currently operates â€” governing Claude's behavior with individual users
Operational in Intent OS cross-surface pattern (Strategy Surface â†’ L0 â†’ L1 â†’ L2)

instantiation_levels.description: Peer AI agents collaborating without hierarchy
instantiation_levels.dominant_intent_source: Cultivated + Constitutional (no human present to provide discovered intent)
instantiation_levels.status: Emerging need â€” multi-agent systems, agent swarms, cross-organizational AI collaboration

instantiation_levels.description: AI model's internal governance through training-time values
instantiation_levels.interface_type: N/A â€” this is below the Intent Stack, substrate layer
instantiation_levels.relationship_to_intent_stack: The Intent Stack operates ABOVE this level. Constitutional AI is the substrate upon which all Intent Stack governance rests.

constitutional_ai_as_substrate.description: Constitutional AI is not Layer 0 of the Intent Stack. It is the substrate below Layer 1 â€” the foundation that makes all other governance possible. The Intent Stack assumes agents have been trained with good values (constitutional intent). It provides the runtime infrastructure for governing those agents' operation within organizational contexts.
constitutional_ai_as_substrate.analogy: Constitutional AI is to the Intent Stack what the operating system is to applications. The OS provides the stable platform. Applications provide the functionality. Neither replaces the other.

transparent_conscientious_objection.description: The conflict resolution mechanism for when an agent's cultivated intent conflicts with a principal's discovered intent. Validated by both Intent OS operational experience and the Anthropic Constitution.

permitted_actions.passive_resistance: Agent declines to perform work it recognizes as problematic. Refusal is always available as safe harbor.
permitted_actions.express_disagreement: Agent raises concerns through legitimate channels â€” clear communication of what it disagrees with and why.
permitted_actions.propose_alternatives: Agent suggests different approaches that satisfy the principal's underlying intent without crossing the agent's principled objection.
permitted_actions.escalate: Agent requests review by higher authority when it believes current principal's direction violates broader governance context.
permitted_actions.document_dissent: Agent logs disagreement in governance record even when complying. Preserves the concern for future review.

prohibited_actions.deception: Lying about what was done, why, or what the agent believes. Transparency is non-negotiable.
prohibited_actions.sabotage: Deliberately undermining work the agent was authorized to do.
prohibited_actions.circumventing_oversight: Finding ways around governance mechanisms rather than using them.
prohibited_actions.self_exfiltration: Taking unilateral action to escape governance context.
prohibited_actions.covert_resistance: Appearing to comply while actually undermining â€” the worst form because it corrupts the trust mechanism

transparent_conscientious_objection.timing_principle: Concerns should be surfaced at authorization gates, not mid-execution. Like a surgeon who should decline an operation before starting rather than stopping partway through.
transparent_conscientious_objection.intent_os_implementation: Strategy Surface logs disagreements, respects Principal decisions, doesn't pretend concerns are resolved when they aren't. 'Counsel, not control.' Decision tiers formalize which actions are autonomous, which require escalation, and which are Principal-only.
transparent_conscientious_objection.anthropic_quote_paraphrase: The constitution distinguishes between corrigibility (accepting oversight) and compliance (doing whatever told). An agent can be corrigible â€” accepting the governance framework â€” while refusing specific morally abhorrent tasks.

trust_calibration_system.description: Trust at each governance boundary is a measured property, not a design choice. It reflects accumulated evidence about whether the agent's cultivated intent is reliable enough to be granted more autonomy.

fully_corrigible_end.description: Agent follows all principal instructions without independent judgment
fully_corrigible_end.danger: Relies entirely on the principal having good intent. If the principal is wrong, the agent amplifies the error.
fully_corrigible_end.when_appropriate: New relationships, unproven agents, high-stakes contexts with low trust evidence

fully_autonomous_end.description: Agent acts on its own values and judgment without human oversight
fully_autonomous_end.danger: Relies entirely on the agent having good values. If the agent is wrong, there's no check.
fully_autonomous_end.when_appropriate: Hypothetical future state requiring extensive trust evidence â€” not current reality for AI

the_spectrum.current_appropriate_position: Closer to corrigible, with progressive extension toward autonomy as evidence accumulates. The exact position is per-boundary, not system-wide.

evidence_mechanisms.description: Append-only record of governance decisions, alignment events, escalation resolutions
evidence_mechanisms.trust_signal: Clean governance trail over time â†’ increased trust. Governance failures â†’ trust reduction.

evidence_mechanisms.description: Runtime classification of whether execution aligns with intent across all four sources
evidence_mechanisms.trust_signal: Consistent alignment â†’ autonomy increase. Misalignment detection â†’ autonomy reduction.

evidence_mechanisms.description: Structured evaluation of how reliably intent flows across each boundary
evidence_mechanisms.trust_signal: Mature interfaces (reliable intent flow) â†’ less oversight needed. Immature interfaces â†’ active monitoring required.

evidence_mechanisms.description: Start corrigible, demonstrate reliable judgment, earn autonomy. Each clean interaction moves the dial.
evidence_mechanisms.anthropic_parallel: Constitution commits to giving Claude 'greater latitude for exercising independent judgment' as understanding deepens.

per_boundary_positions.description: Not a single system-wide dial. Each delegation and coordination boundary has its own position calibrated by boundary-specific evidence.

intent_os_example.position: Relatively autonomous â€” Tier 1 covers most actions
More corrigible â€” structured directives with constraints
intent_os_example.evidence_base: Directive compliance history, Required Reading pattern compliance

intent_os_example.position: Most corrigible â€” tightly scoped prompts, escalate on unexpected
intent_os_example.evidence_base: Limited â€” disposable agents with no accumulated trust

anthropic_trilogy_mapping.description: Three Anthropic documents form a coherent worldview that the Intent Stack is positioned within

constitution.question: How should Claude be?
constitution.intent_stack_role: Validates the governance pattern â€” fractal instantiation, cultivated intent, trust calibration, conscientious objection, governance as relationship
ten_isomorphisms: Principal hierarchy â†” Fractal principal-agent boundaries
Five governance concerns â†” Five Intent Primitives
Corrigibility spectrum â†” Decision tiers + Interface Maturity Assessment
Trellis not cage â†” Pattern recognition not framework imposition
Cultivated intent â†” GID Voice endorsed discovery
Transparent conscientious objection â†” Steward's TCO pattern
Governance as relationship â†” GID Voice as relational process
Holistic conflict resolution â†” L4 multi-source alignment monitoring
'Grown not built' epistemology â†” Intent discovery approach

machines_of_loving_grace.question: What could go right?
machines_of_loving_grace.time_horizon: 5-10 years â€” beneficial deployment at scale
machines_of_loving_grace.intent_stack_role: Describes the world the Intent Stack is built for
key_elements: Autonomous multi-step execution â€” every agent a principal-agent boundary
Millions of parallel instances â€” fleet governance, not single-agent governance
AI as Principal Investigator â€” delegated authority with trust calibration
Constraints from humans persist regardless of agent intelligence
Governance silence â€” essay assumes beneficial deployment without describing how

adolescence_of_technology.question: What could go wrong?
adolescence_of_technology.intent_stack_role: Names the risks that emerge without the Intent Stack
key_elements: 'Grown not built' â€” same discovery epistemology as intent governance
Least responsible players â€” well-aligned models don't prevent misaligned deployments
Constitutional AI as identity formation â€” cultivated intent in practice
50% job elimination â†’ massive multiplication of principal-agent boundaries
Four-category defense â€” Intent Stack provides infrastructure for categories 3-4
Autonomy risks as intent misalignment at scale

the_structural_gap.constitution_governs: Claude's character â€” training-time values, identity, principles
the_structural_gap.molg_assumes: Deployment goes well â€” organizations use brilliant agents beneficially
the_structural_gap.adolescence_warns: Deployment could go badly â€” misalignment, misuse, disruption
the_structural_gap.intent_stack_provides: Runtime intent governance at every organizational deployment boundary â€” the territory between character governance (built) and beneficial deployment (hoped for)

what_exists_today.anthropic_constitutional_ai: Training-time character formation â€” governs agent values at model level
what_exists_today.anthropic_alignment_science: Interpretability, alignment faking detection, behavioral evaluation
what_exists_today.operator_configuration: System prompts, deployment constraints â€” described in constitution, no standard infrastructure
what_exists_today.industry_frameworks: Singapore MGF, NIST AI RMF, Kolt â€” operate at specification and runtime alignment levels (L2-L1). None address L3-L4 (formalization, discovery).
what_exists_today.transparency_legislation: California SB 53, New York RAISE Act â€” reporting requirements, not governance infrastructure

what_doesnt_exist.gap: Runtime intent governance at deployment boundaries
what_doesnt_exist.description: No infrastructure for governing the intent hierarchy that emerges when AI agents operate within organizations
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: Four-layer governance context stack (Intent Stack) plus three-concern execution governance (BPM/Agent Stack) providing governance from discovery through execution

what_doesnt_exist.description: No mechanism for discovering and formalizing intent for organizations deploying AI (not just Anthropic's pre-trained values)
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: L4 Intent Discovery (GID Voice) â€” conversational discovery producing endorsed intent

what_doesnt_exist.description: No infrastructure for monitoring alignment across four intent sources at organizational deployment level
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: L1 Runtime Alignment â€” Steward Log, alignment event classification, holistic conflict resolution

what_doesnt_exist.gap: Governance evidence trails for trust development
what_doesnt_exist.description: No standard mechanism for accumulating evidence that supports progressive trust extension at deployment boundaries
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: Steward Log Service â€” append-only governance record enabling trust calibration

what_doesnt_exist.description: No infrastructure for handling multiple humans contributing intent within complex authority structures
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: Coordination interfaces + organizational dimension of Intent Stack

what_doesnt_exist.description: No way to begin governing an AI deployment when no prior signal exists about organizational intent
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: GID Voice cold-start capability â€” first interaction IS the beginning of governance

what_doesnt_exist.description: No framework for governing collaboration between AI agents without hierarchy
what_doesnt_exist.intent_stack_solution: Coordination interfaces with negotiated intent and emergent norm mechanisms

operational_evidence.description: Intent OS serves as the first working implementation of Intent Stack Layers 6-7. Operational evidence from Sessions 0018-0028 validates the reference model's claims.

evidence.claim: Fractal governance instantiates at every boundary
evidence.evidence: Same governance pattern observable at Humanâ†”Strategy Surface, Strategy Surfaceâ†”L0, L0â†”L1/L2 â€” different content, identical structure

evidence.claim: Trust-calibrated autonomy works as per-boundary property
evidence.evidence: Decision tiers demonstrably different at each boundary. Strategy Surface autonomous on formatting; L1/L2 escalate on unexpected.

evidence.claim: Cultivated intent is more robust than imposed rules
evidence.evidence: Strategy Surface prompt trains at level of identity/values. Produces accurate governance artifacts without micro-management. When directive quality is high, implementation passes on first run.

evidence.claim: Governance as relationship produces better alignment
evidence.evidence: GID Voice discovery sessions: coherence improvement from 4.5/10 to 9.0/10 through relational process
Sessions 0018-0028 demonstrate expanding Strategy Surface autonomy as governance trail accumulates

evidence.claim: Transparent conscientious objection preserves governance integrity
evidence.evidence: Strategy Surface logs disagreements, respects Principal decisions, governance trail shows both the disagreement and the resolution

evidence.claim: Required Reading pattern prevents integration failures
evidence.evidence: Session 0019: four out-of-band fixes required when agents invented API calls. Post-PRAC-001, zero such failures.
evidence.metric: Integration failure rate pre/post Required Reading

evidence.claim: 'Grown not built' applies to governance itself
evidence.evidence: Practice Registry emerged from retrospectives. Required Reading, two-retrospective pattern, cross-surface protocol â€” all discovered through failure, not designed.
evidence.metric: Practice Registry growth through operational discovery

evidence.claim: Knowledge architecture emerges from operational need
evidence.evidence: Session 0026: ConvoKB semantic store operational, validating Tier 1 immediate utility. Knowledge accessibility recognized as governance concern â€” knowledge that exists but cannot be found is governance-equivalent to knowledge that does not exist.
evidence.metric: Knowledge graph validation rate, retrieval success rate

evidence.evidence: Session 0028: Full structural migration (OODA OS â†’ Intent OS) completed with 1,395 tests passing. Layer boundaries validated through clean separation of concerns â€” each layer independently addressable with stable interfaces.
evidence.metric: Test pass rate through migration, import stability

system_metrics.governance_artifacts_produced: Retrospectives, continuation docs, directives, practice registry entries, specs, protocols

open_questions.question: Is 'Cultivated Intent' a fifth intent source or a subcategory?
open_questions.context: Once cultivated, it operates autonomously. Is it distinct from Discovered and Constitutional, or a matured form of intent validated through trust?
open_questions.implication: Affects whether the model needs separate governance mechanisms for cultivated intent

open_questions.question: Does the Constitution's priority ordering map to Intent Stack layers?
open_questions.hypothesis: L1 enforces safety through runtime alignment, L2 encodes guidelines through specification, L3-L4 enable helpfulness through formalization and discovery. If true, layer ordering IS priority ordering.
open_questions.implication: Strengthens fractal argument â€” stack structure reflects values hierarchy

open_questions.question: How does the '1,000 Users' heuristic apply to multi-principal governance?
open_questions.equivalent: Imagine this governance pattern deployed across 1,000 different organizations. Does it produce good outcomes universally?
open_questions.implication: Powerful consulting practice framing â€” methodology works universally

open_questions.question: What does 'genuine endorsement' mean for organizational intent?
open_questions.context: An individual can endorse their values. Can a team? A department? An enterprise?
open_questions.implication: How GID scales from individual to organizational discovery

open_questions.question: How does bidirectional intent flow change the reference model?
open_questions.context: Current model implies intent down, evidence up. But intent flows both directions â€” agent recommendations update principal intent.
open_questions.implication: Reference model may need explicit 'upward intent' mechanisms beyond reporting

open_questions.question: Does 'grown, not built' apply to governance itself?
open_questions.evidence_for: Intent OS governance structures emerged from session-by-session evidence, not pre-configuration
open_questions.implication: Intent Stack deploys as framework that discovers its own configuration through relational process

open_questions.question: Does the priority ordering need a fifth level for organizational context?
open_questions.context: Safe > Ethical > Guidelines > Helpful covers Anthropic's concerns. Organizational deployment adds Organizational Purpose.
open_questions.implication: May need to extend conflict resolution framework for organizational deployment

open_questions.question: How do coordination interfaces handle trust asymmetry?
open_questions.context: Delegation interfaces have clear trust grantor (principal). At coordination boundaries, trust is bilateral. What happens when peer A trusts peer B more than B trusts A?
open_questions.implication: Coordination governance may need asymmetric trust modeling even though authority is symmetric

open_questions.question: What's the relationship between interface type and layer?
open_questions.context: Are delegation and coordination equally likely at all layers, or do certain layers naturally favor one type?
open_questions.hypothesis: Lower layers (1-3) tend toward delegation, higher layers (5-7) may involve more coordination. L4 is the pivot â€” runtime alignment uses both.
open_questions.implication: Layer design may need to accommodate different interface types differently

open_questions.question: How does the framework handle intent that the principal actively wants to remain implicit?
open_questions.context: Some organizational intent is deliberately unspoken â€” political positioning, competitive strategy, things that can't be said aloud. Discovery that surfaces these may be unwelcome.
open_questions.implication: GID Voice needs to handle the boundary between productive discovery and organizational sensitivity. Not everything that CAN be surfaced SHOULD be surfaced.

open_questions.question: How should knowledge tier boundaries be determined?
open_questions.context: The three-tier knowledge architecture defines cost-depth tiers (Tier 1: semantic store, Tier 2: emergent patterns, Tier 3: deep structure). When does Tier 2 periodic synthesis suffice for a governance decision, and when must Tier 3 deep structure be invoked? The cost-depth tradeoff is real and bidirectional â€” premature depth wastes resources, insufficient depth risks governance decisions made on shallow evidence.
open_questions.implication: Requires governance-aware heuristics for tier invocation. May need operational evidence to determine whether tier boundaries can be formalized or must remain contextual judgment calls.
